description: Fig. A.1. Bunching and Annual Changes in Cutoffs. Note: This figure presents the number of applications around admission cutoffs, and year-to-year changes in admission cutoffs. We use the sample of 10,567 students who have a reach–safety programs pair in application years 1994 to 2006. We pool all programs and years and present the distance between the application score and the admission cutoff of the reach program on the x-axis. Panel (a) presents the number of applications around admission cutoffs for the entire sample. Panel (b) presents the difference between the admission cutoff of the student's reach program in the year of application and the cutoff for the same program in the previous year. We restrict the histogram to changes in program admission cutoffs that are between −1 and 1 because they account for most of the data; 1,198 observations are for programs that have an increase in the admission cutoff of greater than 1, while 28 observations are for programs that have a decrease in the admission cutoff of less than −1. Panel (c) shows the year-to-year changes in the admission cutoff of a student's reach program conditional on the student's distance from the admission cutoff for the reach program in the year prior to his or her application.

Fig. A.2. First-stage Results. Note: This figure provides a graphical illustration of the first-stage earnings and earnings gap results. We use the sample of 6,339 students who applied through the Quota 1 system of admission and have a reach–safety programs pair in application years 1994 to 2006. We pool all programs and years and present the distance between the application score and the admission cutoff for the reach program on the x-axis. The results show the coefficients estimated separately for the reach and safety programs using 10 dummy bins centered around the admission cutoff. Each student has exactly one dummy bin variable equal to 1, with the rest equal to zero. In each panel, the dependent variable is a attribute of the earnings process of enrollees in the program of admission, and we plot the coefficients of earnings in the reach and safety programs interacted with distance from cutoff dummies. Earnings are assumed to follow the process described in equation (2). The definitions of the different attributes of the earnings process follow from this equation and are averaged over the period 8 to 12 years after enrollment. Point estimates are given along with the 95% confidence interval.

Fig. A.3. Covariate Balance. Note: This figure presents averages of the covariates used in the analysis by application score bins. We use the sample of 10,567 students who have a reach–safety programs pair in application years 1994 to 2006. We pool all programs and years and present the distance between the application score and the admission cutoff of the reach program on the x-axis. Point estimates are given along with the 95% confidence interval.

Fig. A.4. Proportions of Students in Reach, Safety, or Other Programs Over Time. Note: This figure presents the proportions of students whose latest admission data show that they are enrolled in their (a) reach, (b) safety, or (c) other programs one to six years after their initial admission by application score bins. We pool all programs and present the distance between the student's score and the admission cutoff for the reach program on the x-axis.

Fig. A.5. Switching Conditional on Reach, Safety, or Other Programs. Note: This figure presents the proportions of students whose latest admission data show that they have switched to another program following their initial admission into (a) their reach program, (b) their safety program, (c) another program, or (d) unconditionally one to six years after their initial admission by application score bins. We pool all programs and present the distance between the application score and the admission cutoff for the reach program on the x-axis.

Fig. A.6. Transitions in and out of Reach, Safety, or Other Programs. Note: This figure presents the proportions of students whose latest admission data show that they have transitioned (a) from their reach program to their safety program, (b) from their reach program to another program, (c) from their safety program to their reach program, (d) from their safety program to another program, (e) from another program to their reach program, or (f) from another program to their safety program one to six years after their initial admission by application score bins. We pool all programs and present the distance between the student's score and the admission cutoff for the reach program on the x-axis.
